pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Reads f(x) as multiplication of a quantity f by x instead of the value of function f at input x.
misconceptions.error_signature: For a supplied rule f(x), rewrites f(a) as f times a without substituting a into the rule.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Read the parentheses as an input slot: copy the rule and replace every occurrence of its input variable with the supplied input.

misconceptions.description: Replaces only one occurrence of the function variable in a polynomial rule.
misconceptions.error_signature: For f(x)=a*x^2+b*x+c, substitutes the input into exactly one of the two x terms.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Mark every occurrence of the input variable before substitution, then place the same parenthesized input in every marked location.

misconceptions.description: Drops parentheses around a negative or multi-term input and changes the value of a power or product.
misconceptions.error_signature: For f(t)=a*t+b and input m*x+n, substitutes as a*m*x+n+b rather than a*(m*x+n)+b.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Treat the complete input as one unit by enclosing it in parentheses before applying any operation from the function rule.

metaphors.description: A function rule is a labeled input slot: whatever appears inside the function parentheses is inserted into every matching slot in the rule before calculation begins.

error_patterns: function_notation_as_product: f(a) is treated as f times a and the supplied rule is not used
function_partial_substitution: an input replaces only one occurrence of the function variable
function_grouping_loss: a multi-term input is inserted without parentheses before multiplication or exponentiation
sources: OpenStax College Algebra 2e, Section 3.1: Functions and Function Notation.
College Board AP Precalculus Course and Exam Description, Unit 1: Polynomial and Rational Functions.

lesson_narrative.text: Function notation identifies an input and asks for the output produced by one rule. The symbol inside the parentheses replaces every occurrence of the rule's input variable.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: f of x equals a times x plus b.

lesson_narrative.text: Replace x with the requested input p.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: f of p equals a times p plus b.

lesson_narrative.text: When the input is an expression rather than one number, keep that whole expression grouped during substitution. Simplification happens only after every input slot has been filled.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: f of t equals four times t plus one.
p of x equals three x minus two.
f of p of x equals four times the quantity three x minus two, plus one.

remediation.text: Write the function rule on a fresh line and draw a box around every occurrence of its input variable. Copy the supplied input into every box.

remediation.spoken_text: f of x equals two x squared minus three x plus four.
f of p equals two times p squared minus three times p plus four, with each p grouped.

remediation.text: Before simplifying, count the original variable occurrences and the inserted inputs; the counts must match. Then evaluate powers before multiplication and preserve every sign.

pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Evaluates f(g(x)) in the reverse order by applying f before g.
misconceptions.error_signature: The response to f(g(x)) is algebraically equal to g(f(x)) for the supplied distinct functions.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Start at the innermost parentheses: compute or copy g(x) first, then use that complete output as the input to f.

misconceptions.description: Treats composition notation as a product of function expressions.
misconceptions.error_signature: For f(g(x)), returns f(x)*g(x) or an algebraically equivalent product.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Composition means substitution, not multiplication: one function's complete output occupies the other function's input slot.

misconceptions.description: Substitutes only part of a multi-term inner expression into the outer function.
misconceptions.error_signature: For a quadratic outer rule and affine g(x), squares only the variable term while leaving the inner constant outside the square.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Place parentheses around the entire inner output before applying the outer square, scale, or shift.

metaphors.description: Composition is a pair of nested workstations: the inner station finishes first, and its whole output travels unchanged into the input slot of the outer station.

error_patterns: composition_order_reversed: f(g(x)) is answered with the derived form of g(f(x))
composition_replaced_by_product: f(g(x)) is answered as f(x)*g(x)
composition_inner_ungrouped: an outer power applies to only one term of the affine inner expression
sources: OpenStax College Algebra 2e, Section 3.4: Composition of Functions.
College Board AP Precalculus Course and Exam Description, Unit 1: Function Concepts and Transformations.

lesson_narrative.text: A composition connects two function rules by making one output the next input. Parentheses reveal the order: the innermost function acts first.
Apply g first, then use that complete output as the input to f.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: f of u equals u squared plus three.
g of x equals two x minus one.
f of g of x equals the quantity two x minus one squared, plus three.

lesson_narrative.text: Keep the inner output grouped until the outer rule has been applied. Only then expand or combine terms if the requested answer calls for simplification.

remediation.text: Underline the innermost function name and write its output on a separate line. Draw one box in the outer rule where its input belongs.
Put the complete expression g(x) in the outer input box, then replace every outer variable with that grouped expression.
Check the order by saying it aloud: f of g means g then f. If the response instead multiplies the two original rules or reverses their order, restart from the inner parentheses.

pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Differentiates the outer power but omits multiplication by the inner derivative.
misconceptions.error_signature: For h(x)=(u(x))^n, returns n*(u(x))^(n-1) without a factor equivalent to u'(x).
misconceptions.remediation_hint: After differentiating the outer power, append a separate factor for the derivative of the unchanged inner expression.

misconceptions.description: Returns only the derivative of the inner function and ignores the outer power.
misconceptions.error_signature: For h(x)=(u(x))^n with n greater than one, returns only u'(x).
misconceptions.remediation_hint: The derivative needs both layers: first write n times the unchanged inner expression to power n minus one, then multiply by u prime.

misconceptions.description: Applies the power rule directly to terms inside the grouped inner expression while also reducing the outer exponent.
misconceptions.error_signature: The factor under the reduced outer power is not algebraically equal to the original inner function.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: During the outer step, copy the inner expression unchanged; differentiate that inner expression only in the separate final factor.

metaphors.description: The derivative follows a layered route from outside to inside: transform the outer layer while preserving its input, then multiply by the rate of the inner layer.

error_patterns: chain_missing_inner_factor: n*(u(x))^(n-1) appears without a factor equivalent to u'(x)
chain_inner_only: the response is equivalent to u'(x) and omits the outer-power derivative
chain_inner_copy_changed: the expression beneath the reduced outer power is not equivalent to the original u(x)
sources: OpenStax Calculus Volume 1, Section 3.6: The Chain Rule.
College Board AP Calculus AB and BC Course and Exam Description, Unit 3: Composite, Implicit, and Inverse Functions.

lesson_narrative.text: The chain rule differentiates a composition one layer at a time. For an integer power of an inner function, take the outer power derivative, keep the inner expression unchanged in that outer factor, and multiply by the inner derivative.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: The derivative with respect to x of u of x raised to n equals n times u of x raised to n minus one, times u prime of x.

lesson_narrative.text: Multiply the outer derivative, the unchanged inner form under its reduced power, and the inner derivative.
For this pilot, the inner function is affine or polynomial and the outer exponent is an integer. That keeps every derivative inside the fixed prerequisite graph.

remediation.text: Use three labeled spaces: outer factor, unchanged inner copy, and inner derivative. Do not simplify until all three spaces are filled.

remediation.spoken_text: h of x equals the quantity a x plus b raised to n.
h prime of x equals n times the unchanged quantity a x plus b raised to n minus one, times a.

remediation.text: Compare the grouped inner expression before and after the outer step; those copies must match. Then confirm a separate inner-derivative factor is present before combining constants.
